name: Integrated Output-Capital Ratios and Standard Prices

book_quotes.quote: Figure 9.4 presents the key evidence from United States 1998 data on the paths of individual industry integrated output–capital ratios relative to the output–capital ratio of the standard industry (VRR ≡R). It is clear that individual output–capital ratios VR (r)j generally follow very smooth near-linear paths.

However, four out of the sixty-ﬁve industries do exhibit somewhat more complex movements in the highest range of r/R. Figure 9.5 focuses on these: oil and gas extraction; broadcasting and telecommunications; funds, trusts, and other ﬁnancial vehicles; and food services and drinking places.

This is a linear function passing through the two requisite endpoints: at w = 1, r = 0, we get VR (0)j ≡VR0j = the labor value composition of the jth industry; and at w = 0, r = R, we get VR (R)j = R, which is the labor value composition of the standard industry. The corresponding standard prices of individual commodities p(r)j would then also be exact linear functions.

The following data was taken from the US Bureau of Economics, http://www.bea.gov/iTable/index_industry_io.cfm: industry-by-industry sixty-ﬁve-order total requirements input–output tables B′, after redeﬁnitions designed to match commodity ﬂows to industries; the vector of direct sectoral wage bills W constructed from the Employee Compensation portions of value-added ﬂows in the use tables, after redeﬁnitions; and the market values of industry gross outputs X′ from the same use tables.

Data for total capital stock and total depreciation for private industries in each given year from Fixed Asset Tables 3.1ES (Current-Cost Net Stock of Private Fixed Assets by Industry) and 3.4ES (Current-Cost Depreciation of Private Fixed Assets by Industry), and these were mapped into the sixty-one industries appearing in BEA input–output tables.

primary_source.publication: BEA Industry-by-Industry Use Tables (1998, 65-order) + Fixed Asset Tables 3.1ES (current-cost net stock of private fixed assets by industry) and 3.4ES (current-cost depreciation) + Capital Flow (gross investment) matrix from the 1997 benchmark + NIPA Tables 5.8.5A-B (government investment splits) and 7.5 (Consumption of Fixed Capital by Legal Form) and 7.12 (OOH imputations) + Shaikh (1998a)/Ochoa (1984) historical 71-order IO tables for 1947, 1958, 1963, 1967, 1972
primary_source.table_or_series_id: BEA Use Tables (1998, 65 industries, post-redefinition); Fixed Asset Tables 3.1ES, 3.4ES, 7.1B (government); NIPA Tables 1.10, 5.8.5A-B, 6.4D, 7.5, 7.12; capital flow benchmark matrix (180x123 -> reduced to 65x65) at Annual_IOUse_Before_Redefinitions_1998-2010.xls (columns FO6I-FO9I for government); appendix workbooks Appendix9_1998Circ.xlsx, Appendix9_1998Fixed.xlsx, Appendix9_pvdevexample.xlsx (SalvagedInputs/book_data/ShaikhChoppedTables/)
primary_source.frequency: benchmark cross-section (single year: 1998 for figures 9.4-9.14; multi-year 1947-1998 for Fig 9.15 distance summaries)
primary_source.units: VR(r)_j: integrated output-capital ratio (dimensionless); standard prices p(r)_j/v_j (dimensionless, normalized at p(0)=v); distance measures δc/CV/δe (dimensionless); r/R: dimensionless ratio of actual profit rate to maximum profit rate
primary_source.license: BEA public domain; Shaikh appendix workbooks are book-companion supplementary materials (anwarshaikhecon.org)
primary_source.available_through: BEA iTable / Industry Economic Accounts / Fixed Asset Tables; Shaikh's online appendix at http://www.anwarshaikhecon.org/

formula: VR(r)_j ≡ p(r)_j / (p(r)·KT_(j)); standard price p(r)_j / v_j = 1 + (r/R)·(R - VR0_j)/VR0_j (book eq. 9.20, exact under linear-VR assumption); fixed-capital generalization: p(r) = w·l + p(r)·(A+D) + r·p(r)·K (eq. 9.1.5) with KT ≡ K·(I-(A+D))^-1; standard wage w = 1 - r/R chosen as numeraire

components.label: BEA 1998 Use Table after redefinitions
components.role: 65-order A′ matrix (= I - (B′)^-1) and gross output vector X′

components.label: BEA Employee Compensation rows of value-added block
components.role: direct wage bill W -> skill-adjusted labor coefficients l′

components.label: BEA Fixed Asset Tables 3.1ES (current-cost net stock) and 3.4ES (current-cost depreciation)
components.role: industry-level capital stock K_j and depreciation D_j vectors for the fixed-capital model

components.label: BEA 1997 benchmark capital flow (gross investment) matrix
components.role: supplies inter-industry composition of K_ij by assuming each asset type in industry j grows at the industry's gross growth rate g_j (so capital-stock column proportions = capital-flow column proportions)

components.label: NIPA Tables 5.8.5A-B / 7.5 / 7.1B
components.role: splits government capital stock and depreciation into the four IO-table government cells (Federal/State-Local x General/Enterprise)

components.label: NIPA Table 7.12 lines 133-134
components.role: owner-occupied housing imputations -> real-estate column correction

components.label: Shaikh (1998a) / Ochoa (1984) historical IO tables
components.role: 1947, 1958, 1963, 1967, 1972 71-order matrices (real estate excluded) for Fig 9.13-9.15 multi-year distance series

extension_candidates.subseries_label: BEA Use Tables + Fixed Asset Tables (1997-2023+) annual benchmarks
extension_candidates.source: BEA Industry Economic Accounts and Fixed Assets Accounts
extension_candidates.units: current-cost net stock and depreciation (millions of $); use table flows (millions of $)
concerns: Each benchmark year is a separate cross-section; there is nothing to splice. Adding 2007/2012 would require recomputing the full A′, K, D, KT matrix system and re-running the eigenvalue decomposition for R and p(r).

BEA capital flow matrix is only produced in benchmark years (last published 1997); post-1997 industry composition of capital stock must be inferred from gross investment by industry-by-asset (BEA Detailed Fixed Asset Tables by type and industry)
NAICS classification breaks: pre-1997 SIC tables (Ochoa/Shaikh 1998a) and post-1997 NAICS tables share at most a crude crosswalk

methodology_notes: S902 covers two derived objects sharing the same input data: (i) integrated output-capital ratios VR(r)_j (Fig 9.4, 9.5, 9.9, 9.11) and (ii) standard prices p(r)_j / v_j as functions of r/R (Fig 9.6, 9.7, 9.10, 9.11). Both are evaluated for each of 65 industries in 1998 over r/R ∈ [0,1].

Two models are run separately on the same 1998 data: circulating-capital model (K=A, D=0) and fixed-capital model (K, D constructed from BEA Fixed Asset Tables and the 1997 benchmark capital flow matrix). Figures 9.4-9.8 use circulating; figures 9.9-9.12 use fixed.

Maximum rate of profit R is the reciprocal of the dominant eigenvalue of KT = K·(I-(A+D))^-1 (Appendix 9.1 eq. 9.1.6). Sraffa's standard commodity is implicitly chosen as numeraire (so w = 1 - r/R is exactly linear).

Empirical eigenvalue decomposition (Appendix 9.1 Sec V, eq. 9.1.19) shows p(r) deviates from a linear function only via (r/R)^2 · Σ sub-dominant terms; observed profit shares ~0.30 give (r/R)^2 ≈ 0.09 so the linear Marx component dominates - this is the empirical finding visualized in Fig 9.4-9.7.

Four 'exceptional' industries in Fig 9.5 (oil & gas extraction = industry 3; broadcasting & telecom = 39; funds/trusts/other financial vehicles = 44; food services & drinking places = 60) show non-monotonic VR(r) paths but only in the range r/R > 0.75 and within ±1% of their final value (1).

Distance measures in Fig 9.13/9.14 and Tables 9.12-9.13 quantify the closeness of standard prices to labor times for both models across 1947, 58, 63, 67, 72, 1998 - this is what makes the series multi-year despite each figure being a single-year cross-section.
open_questions: Series count correctness (per Phase 3 Ch9 mandate): S902 lumps 11 figures into one composite 'series'. A defensible alternative would be to split into (a) VR(r)_j ratios as a function of r/R (Fig 9.4, 9.5, 9.9, 9.11), (b) standard-price/labor-time ratios p(r)_j/v_j (Fig 9.6, 9.7, 9.10), (c) standard-price-vs-direct-price distance measures (Fig 9.13, 9.14, 9.15), and (d) production-price-vs-direct-price scatter in 1972 (Fig 9.18). See CH9_RESEARCH_SUMMARY.md for the recommendation.
Should the multi-year distance summary (Fig 9.13/9.15 across 1947-1998) be split off as its own derived time-series-of-cross-sectional-distances? It currently lives partly here and partly in S901.

Capital-flow matrix construction relies on the assumption that all asset types in an industry grow at the same gross rate g_j (Appendix 9.2 Sec IV.1). Validate that BEA's post-1997 detailed Fixed Asset tables (Type x Industry) would allow this assumption to be relaxed in any RSCD replication.
Verify the dominant-eigenvalue numerical recipe in Phase 5/6 against Shaikh's reported R values (Table 9.18: 1947 R=1.088, 1958=0.9734, ..., 1998=0.7317).

review_history.notes: Verbatim quotes from PDF pp.406-407 (Sec VII.1) and Appendix 9.2 pp.867-869. Source identified: BEA Use Tables + Fixed Asset Tables + 1997 capital flow benchmark, plus historical Ochoa/Shaikh (1998a) for 1947-1972. The construction is mathematically explicit (Appendix 9.1).
